title: Write-Review Loops
description: Use you-agent-factory for write → review → revise loops with a bounded retry path.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A write-review loop is a factory workflow where write or execute work produces a candidate, then a review step decides the outcome. Accepted work moves to a complete state. Rejected work returns to write for another pass, so the same goal can revise until review accepts it.

whenToUse.title: When To Use
whenToUse.body: Use this guide when agent output must pass an explicit review gate before completion, for example quality-gated docs, code, or plans. Follow it when you want a clear write → review → revise path in the factory. The outcome you should reach is accepted work that completes only after review, with rejected work routed back for another write pass.

stepsOrWorkflow.title: Steps Or Workflow
stepsOrWorkflow.body: Confirm a live factory, submit or advance write or execute work, let review accept to complete or reject back to write, and bound retries with a loop breaker so reject cycles cannot run forever.

commonPitfalls.title: Common Pitfalls
commonPitfalls.body: Do not review or submit against a factory that is not live. Do not treat reject-without-a-breaker as safe forever. Do not confuse this use-case guide with the later writer-reviewer technique glossary page.

links.startFactoryLabel: Start a factory when no session is active

sessionCheck.body: Start with you or you run when you need a factory, then confirm it with you session list before you submit. If no session is active, start the factory again and re-check.

routing.body: Shape the factory so a write or execute workstation routes successful work into an in-review state for a paired review workstation. On accept, review routes work toward a complete state. On reject, set onRejection to return work to the write or init state for another pass. That execute and review pair, with outcome routes, is the write → review → revise path.

loopBreaker.body: Add a bounded retry so endless reject cycles stop on purpose. A common pattern is a LOGICAL_MOVE guarded by VISIT_COUNT on the write or review workstation: when visits exceed the limit, move the work to a failed or other terminal state instead of sending it around again.

submitAgainstLive.body: Submit only against a running factory. After enqueue, the write or execute workstation produces the candidate; review decides accept or reject.
